So now, I got on my desk in front of me, the five main users of your list, I got their direct mail pieces. So now I can look for commonalities. Same principle, Napoleon Hill, 300 people, what's the 17 commonalities? I can look for the commonalities they're all doing, which it's suggestive, I better have those commonalities in my piece, because that's what all the winners who are using that list have in their piece. So if they're all selling on six-pay, I'm at least going to test six-pay. I don't want to be doing one-pay if every winner using that list is doing six-pay. If they all got photos of gray-haired people, I don't want to use a photo of younger blonde people. Because these are smart people, they've already tested shit. So why do I need to test stuff? I'll just start with their testing. And so most copywriters won't do this work, most marketers won't do this work, but this is how you get to where it pays. Thank you very much. You're welcome. My question is more philosophical than technical. The circulation list versus compile list, I tend to favor all other things being equal. I tend to favor the compiled list over circulation list. And the reason is, the compile list gives two kinds of problems. One is it significantly narrows the spectrum out of a million business owners that only... You mean the circulation list does that. The circulation list is significantly smaller than just because Paul Fireman does not subscribe to this magazine. He's off my radar screen. That's one problem. Second problem that I have a harder time dealing with is the compiled, the circulation data is more heavily marketed to. I would rather reach out to him who is not subscribing to Ink Magazine than the two of them who are subscribing to Ink Magazine because they're going to get eight pieces of mail. He's only going to get mine. What do you think? Well it's going to come down to your niche and who you're going to because I've mailed over 140 million pieces of mail. So I've done a lot of mail. I would say 95% of that has been to response-based lists because I've seen that much difference in them. I have maybe one client where either a controlled universe type file or a compiled file works better. All the other ones response-based works. So the only way to know is to go in there and test and see. I don't think there's a black and white answer for that. Here's the problem with what you're doing. So the scenario you just described has a flaw in the ointment. So you just said you like this guy better who's not on these lists because he's only getting your piece of mail. However it ain't like they didn't try. So he's already been mailed by Ink and by... They already took a whack at him and they couldn't get him. So he is a very stubborn virgin. And seducing stubborn virgin virgins is hard agonizing, long, painful, and usually expensive work. And often generally perhaps not all that productive because they don't know how to behave when you get them. So... Well, I always said that was the dumbest thing. What's this... you get 72 of them or something when you arrive in heaven? First of all, what do you want with 72 of them? And second of all, the last thing I want is... I was like one who knows what the hell she's doing. That'd be more appealing to me. But you know, I mean, the last thing you want to do after a career as a suicide bomber is now have to be a trainer. I mean, you know, just seems to be a bad reward, right? So there's a flaw in your thinking, right? Yeah. I will say this though. So like the wet dream of every direct marketer is how to mail the white pages and make money, okay? And almost everybody takes a whack at it at one time or another, okay? Halbert was able to do it with the name thing. And I'm going to show you a very funny example of that applied to opportunities. And they may be able to do it, right? And I just saw it. LeGrande just sent it to me and it's brilliant. And I can't wait to copy it. But so you really got to rethink this. Say because... Now, the one thing I will say is if you are able to do it, you are... The last part of your logic is true. You are getting to operate for a period of time in essentially a place where you have no competition. And obviously you have bigger numbers, okay? But it ain't easy. The highest probability prospect is always the guy who's already bought X and we can see what motivated him to buy it, right? So like when I sold it to Peter Low events, here's the one commonality I can tell you about almost every buyer, right? 90% of my buyers. They had other people's bags, right? They'd already bought, okay? Non-buyers all left, the buyers stayed, okay? So like my percentage was always fabulous. My closing percentage, my dollars per head number of who was actually in the room, five, six times Ziggs, right? Well, because Ziggs was working with the white pages. He was working with everybody at the start of the day. The only people left to see me were people who were buyers. Well, my percentage damn well better be better, right? You're up, Doc. Hi, how you doing? Thanks for your presentation. It's very good. I have a question. Based on who you're mailing to, do you change your strategy as far as the piece is deliverable? Like here you're selling a course versus going B2B or selling a service? Right. Well, I think, you know, I went through a bunch of different examples up there. So yeah, obviously for every niche there's going to be different format and different style.
